title: Cirrhosis — Decompensated
oneLiner: Liver cirrhosis with acute deterioration (ascites, encephalopathy, variceal bleed, jaundice, coagulopathy). High early mortality, needs rapid triage.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Variceal haemorrhage:** haematemesis/melaena + haemodynamic instability (tachycardia, hypotension). **Single finding:** active haematemesis with a systolic BP <100 mmHg → immediate endoscopic banding plus terlipressin.
- **Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP):** fever + abdominal pain/distension + altered conscious state. **Single finding:** neutrophil count >250 cells/μL on ascitic tap → start empirical antibiotics (e.g., cefotaxime) within 1 hour of tap.
- **Hepatic encephalopathy (HE) grade 3–4:** drowsy/confused + asterixis + flapping tremor → airway compromise imminent. **Single finding:** GCS ≤12 with hyperammonaemia → protect airway (consider ICU) and give lactulose enema/PO.
- **Hepatorenal syndrome (HRS):** oliguria + rising creatinine in setting of ascites, no improvement with volume expansion. **Single finding:** creatinine >133 μmol/L in a cirrhotic with ascites and no other cause → start terlipressin + albumin (do not wait for nephrology).
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Abdominal distension (ascites), leg oedema
  - Yellow skin/eyes (jaundice)
  - Altered behaviour, confusion, drowsiness (HE)
  - Vomiting blood or black/tarry stools (variceal bleed)
  - Fever, rigors, abdominal pain (SBP)
  - Easy bruising, bleeding (coagulopathy)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Spider naevi, palmar erythema, gynaecomastia, testicular atrophy, splenomegaly
  - Asterixis (flapping tremor), fetor hepaticus
  - Shifting dullness, fluid thrill (ascites)
  - Tachycardia, hypotension (hypovolaemia or sepsis)
  - Cool peripheries, low urine output (HRS)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Acute kidney injury from pre-renal causes** → responds to 500 mL of 4.5% human albumin solution; HRS does not.
- **Sepsis from a non-peritoneal source (e.g., UTI, pneumonia)** → localising symptoms + positive blood cultures; no ascitic fluid neutrophil elevation.
- **Acute alcoholic hepatitis** → jaundice, AST:ALT >2, bilirubin >3 mg/dL, history of heavy alcohol use; no acute variceal bleed.
- **Primary biliary cholangitis / decompensated chronic liver disease** → slower onset, anti-mitochondrial antibodies, pruritus, X-ray evidence of biliary disease.
- **Splitter (ascites alone vs SBP):** ascitic fluid polymorphonuclear (PMN) count >250 cells/μL is the definitive split; culture may be negative but treat empirically.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Capillary blood gas (lactate, pH, glucose)
  - Urine dipstick (protein, blood, nitrites)
  - Fingerstick glucose (exclude hypoglycaemia as cause of confusion)
  - Bladder scan (if oliguria – look for retention)
  - NEWS2/MEOWS charting
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT (including INR), CRP, lactate
  - Blood cultures (aerobic + anaerobic) before antibiotics if infection suspected
  - Ammonia (if suspect HE – but treatment should not be delayed for result)
  - Group & save / cross-match if variceal bleed
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** bedside ultrasound (to assess ascites depth, guide paracentesis; look for IVC collapsibility to guide fluid status)
  - **Second-line:** CT abdomen with contrast (if variceal bleed not controlled, or suspect hepatocellular carcinoma, or rule out portal vein thrombosis)
  - **Special:** OGD for variceal bleed (must be performed within 12–24 hours of admission)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**
  - **Child-Pugh score:** class A (5–6), B (7–9), C (10–15) – guides prognosis and treatment intensity.
  - **MELD-Na:** determines 90-day mortality, used for transplant listing. Score >20 suggests high short-term mortality.
- **Never-miss test:** **Diagnostic paracentesis** – must be performed in all new ascites + fever/encephalopathy/abdominal pain. Send for cell count, culture, albumin, and total protein. Omission leads to missed SBP and preventable death.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Establish IV access (two wide-bore cannulae if bleeding)
  - Fluid resuscitation cautiously: 250–500 mL colloid (4.5% albumin) boluses, avoid large volumes of crystalloid (increase ascites)
  - If variceal haemorrhage: terlipressin 2 mg IV stat, then 1–2 mg q4–6h; arrange urgent OGD (within 12 h)
  - If SBP suspected: start empirical antibiotics (e.g., cefotaxime 2 g IV TDS) and give 20% albumin (1.5 g/kg on day 1, 1 g/kg on day 3) – [check local policy for antibiotic choice and dosing]
  - If HE: lactulose 15–30 mL PO (or via NG) TDS, titrate to 2–3 soft stools/day; consider rifaximin 550 mg PO BD if no response
  - If HRS suspected: stop diuretics; give 500 mL 4.5% albumin over 30 minutes; if creatinine still rising, start terlipressin (1 mg IV q4–6h) plus albumin (20 g daily) – [check local protocol for exact doses and duration]
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Monitor daily weights, abdominal girth, urine output, NEWS2
  - Regular LFT, INR, U&E, Mg, PO4
  - Control ascites: spironolactone 100 mg OD + furosemide 40 mg OD (titrate up, target weight loss 0.5–1 kg/day)
  - Malnutrition: refer to dietitian, consider MCT oil, high calorie/protein supplementation (unless hepatic encephalopathy)
  - Antibiotic prophylaxis for variceal bleed? – discuss with senior; often IV ceftriaxone 1 g OD for 5 days
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Abstain from alcohol (liaise with hepatology/addiction services)
  - Hepatology follow-up for transplant assessment if eligible (MELD-Na >15)
  - Regular surveillance: 6-monthly ultrasound + AFP for hepatocellular carcinoma
  - Vaccinations: hepatitis A, B, pneumococcal, influenza
  - Avoid NSAIDs, hepatotoxins, herbal supplements
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 5% dextrose or 50% dextrose with ½ normal saline (avoid large volumes of normal saline – precipitates ascites). Rate: 1 L/24h unless bleeding.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** no – cirrhotics are often coagulopathic; use mechanical compression only unless on anticoagulation.
  - **Diet:** no added salt (<2 g Na/day); high calorie/protein (if no HE); nasogastric feeding if unable to eat.
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 + urine output chart + daily weight.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Ascites – first-line:** Spironolactone 100 mg PO OD, increase to 400 mg if needed; add furosemide 40 mg PO OD if poor response.
- **Hepatic encephalopathy – first-line:** Lactulose 15–30 mL PO TDS (titrate to 2–3 soft stools/day). If intolerant or refractory: rifaximin 550 mg PO BD.
- **Variceal haemorrhage:** Terlipressin 2 mg IV stat, then 1–2 mg IV q4–6h (max 3 days). Always given with prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone 1 g IV OD for 5 days) and vitamin K 10 mg IV.
- **SBP:** Empirical broad-spectrum – cefotaxime 2 g IV TDS for 5–7 days. Add 20% albumin (1.5 g/kg on day 1, 1 g/kg on day 3) to reduce renal impairment.
- **HRS (type 1):** Terlipressin 1 mg IV q4–6h (increase to 2 mg q4–6h if non-response after 3 days) plus albumin (20 g IV daily). [Check local protocol for duration and monitoring.]
- **Key interactions / contraindications:**
  - Spironolactone + ACEi → hyperkalaemia. Monitor K+ closely.
  - Lactulose – can cause severe diarrhoea, electrolyte disturbance.
  - Terlipressin – ischaemic events (cardiac, mesenteric) if high dose or prolonged.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Most drugs need dose reduction; always check BNF for hepatotoxicity. For example, cefotaxime – reduce dose in severe renal impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Spironolactone** – starting at >100 mg daily without monitoring U&E can cause life-threatening hyperkalaemia. **Terlipressin** – giving without confirming active variceal bleed may cause unnecessary ischaemia. **Lactulose** – over-treating (more than 4–5 stools/day) → severe fluid and electrolyte loss.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Variceal rebleeding (within 5 days)
  - Hepatic encephalopathy (grade 3–4 needing ICU)
  - Hepatorenal syndrome (type 1 – rapidly progressive AKI)
  - Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis → bacteraemia, sepsis, multi-organ failure
  - Acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF) with extrahepatic organ failures
- **Chronic:**
  - Hepatocellular carcinoma (develops in up to 30% of cirrhotics)
  - Portal vein thrombosis
  - Malnutrition, sarcopenia, hepatic osteodystrophy
  - Refractory ascites (needs TIPS or transplant)
sections.Prognosis:
- **Short-term mortality** in decompensated cirrhosis: Child-Pugh C → 50–80% 1-year mortality without transplant.
- **Variceal haemorrhage:** in-hospital mortality ~15–20% (improved with early intervention).
- **HRS type 1:** median survival <1 month without treatment; transplant offers only cure.
- **Child-Pugh B / MELD 10–15** may stabilise with medical management and alcohol cessation.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always perform a diagnostic paracentesis** in any decompensated cirrhotic with fever, abdominal pain, or altered mental state — even if ascites seems small.
- **Give prophylactic antibiotics** for any variceal bleed (ceftriaxone 1 g IV OD for 5 days) to prevent infection and rebleeding.
- **Titrate lactulose to 2–3 soft stools/day** — too many stools causes dehydration and worsens HE; too few gives no effect.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give large-volume normal saline** — it expands ascites, worsens oedema, and does not improve intravascular volume. Use colloid (albumin) or dextrose.
- **Do not delay variceal banding** for coagulopathy correction — platelets/FFP are not needed for OGD unless INR >3 and bleeding ongoing.
- **Do not use spironolactone in HRS or acute kidney injury** — it may cause hyperkalaemia and volume overload; stop all diuretics in acute renal impairment.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** any new haematemesis/melaena, worsening confusion (unable to rouse), abdominal pain with fever, reduced urine output (<400 mL/day), or jaundice deepening.
- **Follow-up:** Hepatology clinic within 2 weeks; alcohol liaison referral as needed; repeat LFT and U&E in 7 days; plan for HCC surveillance (6-monthly ultrasound ± AFP).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (Registrar/SpR) immediately if:**
  - Active variceal bleed with systolic BP <90 mmHg despite initial terlipressin and fluids
  - GCS ≤12 in HE (urgent CT head to rule out bleed; consider ICU for airway protection)
  - Creatinine rising >50 μmol/L per day despite volume expansion (possible HRS)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L or base deficit >6 (sepsis/hypoperfusion)
  - NEWS2 ≥7 (any red parameter) — especially respiratory rate >25 or O2 sats <90%
  - First episode of paracentesis with neutrophil >250 cells/μL (SBP) — do not delay antibiotics while waiting for senior review
- **Consider ICU referral if:**
  - Need for vasopressors (persistent hypotension despite fluid)
  - GCS ≤10 (airway risk)
  - Continuous renal replacement therapy anticipated

rawMarkdown:
## Cirrhosis — Decompensated
**One-liner:** Liver cirrhosis with acute deterioration (ascites, encephalopathy, variceal bleed, jaundice, coagulopathy). High early mortality, needs rapid triage.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Variceal haemorrhage:** haematemesis/melaena + haemodynamic instability (tachycardia, hypotension). **Single finding:** active haematemesis with a systolic BP <100 mmHg → immediate endoscopic banding plus terlipressin.
- **Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP):** fever + abdominal pain/distension + altered conscious state. **Single finding:** neutrophil count >250 cells/μL on ascitic tap → start empirical antibiotics (e.g., cefotaxime) within 1 hour of tap.
- **Hepatic encephalopathy (HE) grade 3–4:** drowsy/confused + asterixis + flapping tremor → airway compromise imminent. **Single finding:** GCS ≤12 with hyperammonaemia → protect airway (consider ICU) and give lactulose enema/PO.
- **Hepatorenal syndrome (HRS):** oliguria + rising creatinine in setting of ascites, no improvement with volume expansion. **Single finding:** creatinine >133 μmol/L in a cirrhotic with ascites and no other cause → start terlipressin + albumin (do not wait for nephrology).

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Abdominal distension (ascites), leg oedema
  - Yellow skin/eyes (jaundice)
  - Altered behaviour, confusion, drowsiness (HE)
  - Vomiting blood or black/tarry stools (variceal bleed)
  - Fever, rigors, abdominal pain (SBP)
  - Easy bruising, bleeding (coagulopathy)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Spider naevi, palmar erythema, gynaecomastia, testicular atrophy, splenomegaly
  - Asterixis (flapping tremor), fetor hepaticus
  - Shifting dullness, fluid thrill (ascites)
  - Tachycardia, hypotension (hypovolaemia or sepsis)
  - Cool peripheries, low urine output (HRS)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Acute kidney injury from pre-renal causes** → responds to 500 mL of 4.5% human albumin solution; HRS does not.
- **Sepsis from a non-peritoneal source (e.g., UTI, pneumonia)** → localising symptoms + positive blood cultures; no ascitic fluid neutrophil elevation.
- **Acute alcoholic hepatitis** → jaundice, AST:ALT >2, bilirubin >3 mg/dL, history of heavy alcohol use; no acute variceal bleed.
- **Primary biliary cholangitis / decompensated chronic liver disease** → slower onset, anti-mitochondrial antibodies, pruritus, X-ray evidence of biliary disease.
- **Splitter (ascites alone vs SBP):** ascitic fluid polymorphonuclear (PMN) count >250 cells/μL is the definitive split; culture may be negative but treat empirically.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Capillary blood gas (lactate, pH, glucose)
  - Urine dipstick (protein, blood, nitrites)
  - Fingerstick glucose (exclude hypoglycaemia as cause of confusion)
  - Bladder scan (if oliguria – look for retention)
  - NEWS2/MEOWS charting
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT (including INR), CRP, lactate
  - Blood cultures (aerobic + anaerobic) before antibiotics if infection suspected
  - Ammonia (if suspect HE – but treatment should not be delayed for result)
  - Group & save / cross-match if variceal bleed
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** bedside ultrasound (to assess ascites depth, guide paracentesis; look for IVC collapsibility to guide fluid status)
  - **Second-line:** CT abdomen with contrast (if variceal bleed not controlled, or suspect hepatocellular carcinoma, or rule out portal vein thrombosis)
  - **Special:** OGD for variceal bleed (must be performed within 12–24 hours of admission)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**
  - **Child-Pugh score:** class A (5–6), B (7–9), C (10–15) – guides prognosis and treatment intensity.
  - **MELD-Na:** determines 90-day mortality, used for transplant listing. Score >20 suggests high short-term mortality.
- **Never-miss test:** **Diagnostic paracentesis** – must be performed in all new ascites + fever/encephalopathy/abdominal pain. Send for cell count, culture, albumin, and total protein. Omission leads to missed SBP and preventable death.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Establish IV access (two wide-bore cannulae if bleeding)
  - Fluid resuscitation cautiously: 250–500 mL colloid (4.5% albumin) boluses, avoid large volumes of crystalloid (increase ascites)
  - If variceal haemorrhage: terlipressin 2 mg IV stat, then 1–2 mg q4–6h; arrange urgent OGD (within 12 h)
  - If SBP suspected: start empirical antibiotics (e.g., cefotaxime 2 g IV TDS) and give 20% albumin (1.5 g/kg on day 1, 1 g/kg on day 3) – [check local policy for antibiotic choice and dosing]
  - If HE: lactulose 15–30 mL PO (or via NG) TDS, titrate to 2–3 soft stools/day; consider rifaximin 550 mg PO BD if no response
  - If HRS suspected: stop diuretics; give 500 mL 4.5% albumin over 30 minutes; if creatinine still rising, start terlipressin (1 mg IV q4–6h) plus albumin (20 g daily) – [check local protocol for exact doses and duration]
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Monitor daily weights, abdominal girth, urine output, NEWS2
  - Regular LFT, INR, U&E, Mg, PO4
  - Control ascites: spironolactone 100 mg OD + furosemide 40 mg OD (titrate up, target weight loss 0.5–1 kg/day)
  - Malnutrition: refer to dietitian, consider MCT oil, high calorie/protein supplementation (unless hepatic encephalopathy)
  - Antibiotic prophylaxis for variceal bleed? – discuss with senior; often IV ceftriaxone 1 g OD for 5 days
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Abstain from alcohol (liaise with hepatology/addiction services)
  - Hepatology follow-up for transplant assessment if eligible (MELD-Na >15)
  - Regular surveillance: 6-monthly ultrasound + AFP for hepatocellular carcinoma
  - Vaccinations: hepatitis A, B, pneumococcal, influenza
  - Avoid NSAIDs, hepatotoxins, herbal supplements
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 5% dextrose or 50% dextrose with ½ normal saline (avoid large volumes of normal saline – precipitates ascites). Rate: 1 L/24h unless bleeding.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** no – cirrhotics are often coagulopathic; use mechanical compression only unless on anticoagulation.
  - **Diet:** no added salt (<2 g Na/day); high calorie/protein (if no HE); nasogastric feeding if unable to eat.
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 + urine output chart + daily weight.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Ascites – first-line:** Spironolactone 100 mg PO OD, increase to 400 mg if needed; add furosemide 40 mg PO OD if poor response.
- **Hepatic encephalopathy – first-line:** Lactulose 15–30 mL PO TDS (titrate to 2–3 soft stools/day). If intolerant or refractory: rifaximin 550 mg PO BD.
- **Variceal haemorrhage:** Terlipressin 2 mg IV stat, then 1–2 mg IV q4–6h (max 3 days). Always given with prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone 1 g IV OD for 5 days) and vitamin K 10 mg IV.
- **SBP:** Empirical broad-spectrum – cefotaxime 2 g IV TDS for 5–7 days. Add 20% albumin (1.5 g/kg on day 1, 1 g/kg on day 3) to reduce renal impairment.
- **HRS (type 1):** Terlipressin 1 mg IV q4–6h (increase to 2 mg q4–6h if non-response after 3 days) plus albumin (20 g IV daily). [Check local protocol for duration and monitoring.]
- **Key interactions / contraindications:**
  - Spironolactone + ACEi → hyperkalaemia. Monitor K+ closely.
  - Lactulose – can cause severe diarrhoea, electrolyte disturbance.
  - Terlipressin – ischaemic events (cardiac, mesenteric) if high dose or prolonged.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Most drugs need dose reduction; always check BNF for hepatotoxicity. For example, cefotaxime – reduce dose in severe renal impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Spironolactone** – starting at >100 mg daily without monitoring U&E can cause life-threatening hyperkalaemia. **Terlipressin** – giving without confirming active variceal bleed may cause unnecessary ischaemia. **Lactulose** – over-treating (more than 4–5 stools/day) → severe fluid and electrolyte loss.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Variceal rebleeding (within 5 days)
  - Hepatic encephalopathy (grade 3–4 needing ICU)
  - Hepatorenal syndrome (type 1 – rapidly progressive AKI)
  - Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis → bacteraemia, sepsis, multi-organ failure
  - Acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF) with extrahepatic organ failures
- **Chronic:**
  - Hepatocellular carcinoma (develops in up to 30% of cirrhotics)
  - Portal vein thrombosis
  - Malnutrition, sarcopenia, hepatic osteodystrophy
  - Refractory ascites (needs TIPS or transplant)

### Prognosis
- **Short-term mortality** in decompensated cirrhosis: Child-Pugh C → 50–80% 1-year mortality without transplant.
- **Variceal haemorrhage:** in-hospital mortality ~15–20% (improved with early intervention).
- **HRS type 1:** median survival <1 month without treatment; transplant offers only cure.
- **Child-Pugh B / MELD 10–15** may stabilise with medical management and alcohol cessation.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always perform a diagnostic paracentesis** in any decompensated cirrhotic with fever, abdominal pain, or altered mental state — even if ascites seems small.
- **Give prophylactic antibiotics** for any variceal bleed (ceftriaxone 1 g IV OD for 5 days) to prevent infection and rebleeding.
- **Titrate lactulose to 2–3 soft stools/day** — too many stools causes dehydration and worsens HE; too few gives no effect.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give large-volume normal saline** — it expands ascites, worsens oedema, and does not improve intravascular volume. Use colloid (albumin) or dextrose.
- **Do not delay variceal banding** for coagulopathy correction — platelets/FFP are not needed for OGD unless INR >3 and bleeding ongoing.
- **Do not use spironolactone in HRS or acute kidney injury** — it may cause hyperkalaemia and volume overload; stop all diuretics in acute renal impairment.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** any new haematemesis/melaena, worsening confusion (unable to rouse), abdominal pain with fever, reduced urine output (<400 mL/day), or jaundice deepening.
- **Follow-up:** Hepatology clinic within 2 weeks; alcohol liaison referral as needed; repeat LFT and U&E in 7 days; plan for HCC surveillance (6-monthly ultrasound ± AFP).

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (Registrar/SpR) immediately if:**
  - Active variceal bleed with systolic BP <90 mmHg despite initial terlipressin and fluids
  - GCS ≤12 in HE (urgent CT head to rule out bleed; consider ICU for airway protection)
  - Creatinine rising >50 μmol/L per day despite volume expansion (possible HRS)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L or base deficit >6 (sepsis/hypoperfusion)
  - NEWS2 ≥7 (any red parameter) — especially respiratory rate >25 or O2 sats <90%
  - First episode of paracentesis with neutrophil >250 cells/μL (SBP) — do not delay antibiotics while waiting for senior review
- **Consider ICU referral if:**
  - Need for vasopressors (persistent hypotension despite fluid)
  - GCS ≤10 (airway risk)
  - Continuous renal replacement therapy anticipated
